text: The surgical team begins the gastrectomy with four robotic ports positioned 16 to 18 centimeters caudal to the xiphoid. An 8-centimeter liver retractor is used for exposure. Arm one holds a cardiac grasper, arm two a bipolar vessel sealer, arm three contains the camera positioned just off the midline, and arm four has a fenestrated bipolar. A laparoscopic assistant port is placed between arms three and four, left as a 12-millimeter port for potential stapler use. The lesser sac is entered opposite the incisura, and a lighted 40-fence bougie with a gastro-protraction device is advanced into the stomach. A sponge is inserted early and left in the lesser sac to aid exposure.

The surgical team sets up four robotic ports, positioned 16 to 18 centimeters caudal to the xiphoid, using an 8-centimeter retractor for liver exposure. Arm one holds a cardiac grasper, arm two a bipolar vessel sealer, arm three contains the camera positioned just off the midline, and arm four has a fenestrated bipolar. A laparoscopic assistant port is placed between arms three and four, left as a 12-millimeter port for potential stapler use. The lesser sac is entered opposite the incisura, and a lighted 40-french bougie with a gastro-protraction device is advanced into the stomach. A sponge is inserted early and left in the lesser sac for exposure aid.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach. Posterior attachments to the pancreas and retroperitoneum are carefully dissected without energy use. The dissection proceeds towards the angle of His to expose the left crus, ensuring no significant hernia is present. The gastro-sail retraction device is adjusted by withdrawing the sail portion while leaving the bougie in place.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels along the greater curve, carefully dissecting posterior attachments to the pancreas and retroperitoneum without using energy. The dissection proceeds to the angle of His to expose the left crus and check for hernias, while avoiding injury to the gastropathic ligament and left gastric vessels. The gastro-sail retraction device is adjusted to prevent obstruction, leaving the bougie in place.

The surgical team exposes the entire length of the left crus, confirming the absence of a hiatal hernia. After ensuring no esophageal temp probes or gastric tubes are present, they begin stapling opposite the incisura to create a four-centimeter wide channel. The bougie is not used as a guide for the first staple fire to avoid anatomical distortion, but it is advanced after clamping the tissue to ensure no obstruction.

The surgical team exposes the entire length of the left crus, confirming the absence of a hiatal hernia. They begin stapling opposite the incisura to create a four-centimeter wide channel, avoiding the use of the bougie as a guide to prevent anatomical distortion. Green loads are used for the initial staple fire, and areas of bleeding are oversewn with 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon begins stapling the stomach using green loads, ensuring the staple line remains flat to prevent corkscrewing. The greater curvature of the stomach is retracted laterally as the team progresses towards the angle of His. The bougie is carefully maneuvered to avoid compressing the tissue, and areas of bleeding are oversewn with 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon retracts the greater curve of the stomach laterally while advancing towards the angle of His. The stomach is kept flat to avoid corkscrewing the staple line, and the bougie is positioned close but not squeezed by the tissue. Green loads are used for stapling along the stomach's length, with the bougie being slid back and forth to maintain proper distance during staple firing.

The surgeon performs a leak test by injecting 100 cc of methylene blue mixed with saline through the aspiration channel on the bougie, observing for bubbles under saline to confirm the absence of leaks. Tissue sealant is applied along the staple line, and the specimen is extracted. The fascia is then closed.
